title: Lavender bath salts
subtitle: Dead Sea salt blend with lavender essential oil and dried flowers
excerpt: A lavender bath salt blend using Dead Sea salt and Epsom salt as the mineral base, with lavender essential oil and dried lavender flowers. The Epsom salt provides magnesium; the Dead Sea salt provides trace minerals. A single recipe fills one 500 g jar.
sourceNotes: Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014), p. 327, lavender in rinse-off bath preparations. Rosemary Gladstar, Herbal Recipes for Vibrant Health (2008), bath salt ratios and magnesium salt notes.

recipe.batchNotes: Scales directly. Double or triple the recipe and store in sealed jars.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready immediately. Keeps 12 months in an airtight jar.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months in an airtight jar. Salt naturally absorbs moisture; a clumped jar is not spoiled, just humid. Break up clumps with a spoon. Discard if the essential oil scent has faded completely or if any discolouration appears on the dried flowers.

content.text: A lavender bath salt blend. Dead Sea salt and Epsom salt (magnesium sulphate) in a 3:2 ratio; Dead Sea salt provides the mineral profile, Epsom salt the magnesium. Lavender essential oil at 1% of the salt weight and dried lavender flowers for the visual. The recipe fills one 500 g jar and provides 5-10 baths depending on how much is used per bath.

Lavender essential oil is one of the few essential oils considered safe for bath use at normal concentrations; it disperses through the bath water rather than sitting on the surface in concentrated drops. Do not add undiluted essential oils directly to bath water; the oil floats as concentrated droplets and can cause skin sensitisation.

items.prepNote: coarse Dead Sea salt or coarse sea salt. 60% of the batch. Coarse grain holds the fragrance better than fine.
Epsom salt (magnesium sulphate). 40% of the batch. Available from pharmacies. Finer grain than Dead Sea salt.

Lavandula angustifolia lavender essential oil. 1% of the salt weight (approximately 5 g ≈ 5 ml). Carrier oil optional: mixing the EO with 5 ml sweet almond oil before adding to the salts helps it disperse more evenly and prevents the oil from pooling at the bottom.

content.text: Weigh the Dead Sea salt and Epsom salt into a bowl and stir to combine. The two have different crystal sizes; stir until visually mixed.
Add the essential oil and stir

If using a carrier oil, mix the lavender essential oil with 5 ml of sweet almond oil in a small cup first. Drizzle the essential oil (or the oil blend) over the salt mixture in a thin stream, stirring continuously to coat all the salt crystals as evenly as possible. The salt will absorb the oil quickly; continue stirring for 1 minute.

Spoon the bath salts into a clean, dry jar. If using dried lavender flowers, scatter them on top of the salts in the jar as a visual layer; they do not mix into the salts well but sit attractively on the surface. Seal the jar. Allow the fragrance to infuse the salts for at least 24 hours before using for best scent.
Scoop 3-5 tablespoons (approximately 50-80 g) into a running bath. Stir to help dissolve. Soak for 15-20 minutes. Shower off afterwards to remove the salt residue.

12 months in an airtight jar in a cool, dry location. Salt is hygroscopic and absorbs moisture from the air; keep the lid on the jar when not in use. Clumping is normal in a humid bathroom; break clumps with a spoon. The lavender essential oil scent fades over 12 months; a drop of fresh essential oil can refresh an older batch.
